coredata.dc:title: Novel fluorescent carbonized polymer dots with clear core–shell structure derived from N-hydroxymethylacrylamide and its polymer for facile and sensitive sensing of tetracycline
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Zixun
Wang, Hailong
Zeng, Yanbo
Yang, Yiwen
Zhang, Zulei
Huang, Hong
Hu, Hongyu
Li, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), as a novel type of carbon-based luminescence nanomaterials, have attracted great attentions in recent years. The microstructure, formation progress, photoluminescence mechanism, and practical application required to be further explored and illustrated. In this work, two novel CPDs, denoted as CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA, were synthesized from N-hydroxymethylacrylamide and its polymer. The core–shell morphology of the CPDs was clearly observed for the first time. The as-prepared CPDs exhibited intriguing excitation wavelength and concentration dependent photoluminescent characteristics. Spectral characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) investigations revealed that two-dimensional rigid structure formed by vinyl polymerization and hydroxymethyl crosslinking, together with inter/intramolecular hydrogen bonding interaction, resulted in a remarkable fluorescence emission. The aggregation of CPDs through hydrogen bonding interaction originated from the carbonyl and amino groups at the polymer layer contributed to the emission wavelengths shifted to long wavelength with increasing of the concentration of CPDs. A facile analysis method for tetracycline (TC) detection was constructed based on the CPDs fluorescence with advantages of rapid response, high sensitivity, good selectivity, and wide linear range. Moreover, this approach has been applicated in determination of TC in environmental water samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Core–shell microstructure
Formation and photoluminescence mechanism
Fluorescence sensing

Analytical performance and possible sensing mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical characterization of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA. (A) FTIR spectra, (B) Raman spectra, (C) 1H NMR spectra, and (D) 13C NMR spectra.

XPS spectra of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA. CPDs-HMA: (A) XPS survey full spectra, (B)–(D) high-solution XPS C1s, N1s and O1s. CPDs-PHMA: (E) XPS survey full spectra, (F)–(H) high-solution XPS C1s, N1s and O1s.
HR-TEM images and size distribution of (A–C) CPDs-HMA and (D–F) CPDs-PHMA.
Maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of CPDs-HMA (A, B) and CPDs-PHMA (C, D) changing with the concentration from 0.025 mg/mL to 15.0 mg/mL.
DFT investigation of the formation progress and luminescence mechanism (A) and the concentration dependent luminescence property (B).

Variations of fluorescence spectra of the CPDs with the TC concentration, linear equation between F0/F − 1 with the concentration of TC, normalized absorption spectrum of TC (60.0 μM), excitation and emission spectra, and fluorescence lifetime decay curve in the absence and presence of TC (60.0 μM). (A–D) CPDs-HMA and (E–H) CPDs-PHMA.

Fluorescence quenching efficiencies, normalized absorption spectrum of TC and structure analogues, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, and UV–vis absorption spectra of the CPDs with inorganic ions. (A–C) CPDs-HMA and (D–E) CPDs-PHMA. The concentration of TC was 20.0 μM and the potential interfering substances were 3-fold concentration of TC.
Schematic illustration: (A) preparation of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA, (B) fluorescent detection of TC based on FRET mechanism.
Analysis performance of the CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA to TC in real water, the data listed within the bracket were determined from the CPDs-PHMA.

head.ce:title: Novel fluorescent carbonized polymer dots with clear core–shell structure derived from N-hydroxymethylacrylamide and its polymer for facile and sensitive sensing of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biological, Chemical Sciences and Engineering, Jiaxing Key Laboratory of Molecular Recognition and Sensing, Jiaxing University, Jiaxing 314001 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiaxing Key Laboratory of Molecular Recognition and Sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biological, Chemical Sciences and Engineering, Jiaxing Key Laboratory of Molecular Recognition and Sensing, Jiaxing University, Jiaxing 314001, PR China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The core-shell microstructures of the CPDs derived from N-hydroxymethylacrylamide and its polymer were clearly observed for the first time. Two-dimensional network structure formed by vinyl polymerization and hydroxymethyl crosslinking, together with the inter/intramolecular hydrogen bonding interaction, resulted in a remarkable fluorescence emission. A facile and sensitive analysis method for tetracycline detection was established based on the CPDs fluorescence with satisfactory results.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), as a novel type of carbon-based luminescence nanomaterials, have attracted great attentions in recent years. The microstructure, formation progress, photoluminescence mechanism, and practical application required to be further explored and illustrated. In this work, two novel CPDs, denoted as CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA, were synthesized from N-hydroxymethylacrylamide and its polymer. The core–shell morphology of the CPDs was clearly observed for the first time. The as-prepared CPDs exhibited intriguing excitation wavelength and concentration dependent photoluminescent characteristics. Spectral characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) investigations revealed that two-dimensional rigid structure formed by vinyl polymerization and hydroxymethyl crosslinking, together with inter/intramolecular hydrogen bonding interaction, resulted in a remarkable fluorescence emission. The aggregation of CPDs through hydrogen bonding interaction originated from the carbonyl and amino groups at the polymer layer contributed to the emission wavelengths shifted to long wavelength with increasing of the concentration of CPDs. A facile analysis method for tetracycline (TC) detection was constructed based on the CPDs fluorescence with advantages of rapid response, high sensitivity, good selectivity, and wide linear range. Moreover, this approach has been applicated in determination of TC in environmental water samples with satisfactory results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: CPDs, as a novel type of carbon-based luminescence nanomaterials, possess a unique hybrid structure of partially carbonized core and crosslinking polymer shell bearing abundant amino, hydroxyl, and carbonyl functional groups [1,2], which provides them with outstanding luminescence properties. CPDs not only preserve the characteristics of low toxicity and good water solubility of carbon dots (CDs), but also possess the properties of high photo-stabling and easy modification of polymer dots (PDs) [3]. Combining these advantages makes CPDs have great potential applications in chemosensing [4], bioimaging [5], and luminescent devices [6].

Currently, CPDs were usually synthesized from small molecule [7] and polymer [8] through polymerization, crosslinking, and carbonization by using hydrothermal [9], solvothermal [10], and microwave methods [11]. Carbon core state and surface state [12], molecular state [13], and crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) [14] has been proposed to illustrate the photoluminescence mechanism of the CPDs. Besides the synthesis conditions, the morphology, size distribution, and luminescence performance were strongly influenced by the precursors [15]. Compared with the polymer precursor, small molecule precursor containing vinyl group can realize the effective polymerization, crosslinking, carbonization, and efficiently convert into CPDs with uniform size, which makes them to be an ideal precursor for the CPDs synthesis [16]. Up to now, CPDs with excellent luminescence properties have been synthesized [17–19], which generally focus on the optical properties and applications. There are some issues need to be addressed [20], such as the microstructure and formation progress need to be deeply elucidated, the photoluminescence mechanism require further illustration, and the proposed core–shell structure has not been clearly observed. Thereby, deep insight into the formation and photoluminescence mechanism could contribute to rational design and synthesis of the CPDs.

Tetracycline (TC), one of the most used broad-spectrum antibiotics, exhibited excellent antibacterial ability against gram positive and negative bacteria, mycoplasma, and chlamydia. Long-time and excessive application of tetracycline have brought about the residue accumulation in the environment, which exerted a serious threat to human health. So far, some analytical methods including Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [21], capillary electrophoresis [22], high-performance liquid chromatography [23], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [24], and fluorescence spectrometry [25] have been established for TC detection. Among them, fluorescence method has advantages of simplicity, rapid response, high selectivity and sensitivity [26]. Therefore, developing facile fluorescence method based on novel nanomaterial was expected to meet the urgent demand of convenient detection of TC.

Motivated by these considerations, as shown in Scheme 1 , the novel CPDs denoted as CPDs-HMA were synthesized from N-hydroxymethylacrylamide (HMA). Furthermore, Poly (N-hydroxymethylacrylamide) (PHMA) was prepared from HMA under acidic condition, then, the CPDs-PHMA was synthesized from PHMA which was intended to help revealing the formation progress of CPDs-HMA from HMA. The probable microstructure, formation progress, and photoluminescence mechanism were explored and illustrated by spectral characterizations and DFT investigations. As a novel fluorescence probe, the CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA displayed an excellent sensing ability to TC response based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism, and has been successfully applied to the determination of TC in water samples with satisfactory results.
The details of chemicals and instruments are available in the Supplementing materials.

One-pot hydrothermal method was employed to prepare CPDs-HMA. Briefly, 0.5050 g (0.5 mmol) HMA was dissolved in 15 mL deionized water. Then, the solution was transferred into 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The obtained brown–red solution was dialyzed with 500 Da for 24 h against deionized water to remove unreacted reactants. Finally, the dialyzed solution was concentrated and lyophilized for obtaining a black-brown solid powder.

PHMA and CPDs-PHMA were synthesized as following: 0.5050 g (0.5 mmol) HMA was completely dissolved into 15 mL dilute sulfuric acid solution (pH = 4.0). Then, the mixture was heated with refluxing at 120 °C for 24 h. The final white solid powder was obtained by filtering and vacuum drying at 60 °C overnight. 0.5050 g PHMA and 15 mL deionized water were added into 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave. Then, CPDs-PHMA was synthesized by the procedures as described for the CPDs-HMA synthesis.

The relative fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA were calculated as follows:
QY S
=
QY R
×
m S
/
m R
×
η
S
2
/
η
R
2
. QY was the quantum yield, m denoted the slope of the integrated fluorescence intensity against the absorbance of the CPDs. The subscripts “S” and “R” represented the samples (CPDs-HMA or CPDs-PHMA) and the reference material. ηR and ηs was the refractive index of the solvent used for sample and reference, the value of ηs/ηR was approximately equal to 1.0. The reference material was quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 (QY = 55 %).

In order to evaluated the biocompatibility of the CPDs, the cytotoxicity of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA were measured by using an MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazo-lium bromide) method. The specific steps were as follows: First, the Hela cells were incubated in 96-well flat-bottomed plates (4000–5000 cells per well) at 37 °C overnight. Next, the cells were incubated with different concentrations of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA for 48 h. Following exposure, 30 μL MTT (5.0 mg/mL in PBS solution) was added into each well. Then, the MTT solution was removed from the wells after 4.0 h, and the purple MTT-formazan crystals were dissolved by adding 100 μL DMSO. Finally, the absorbance of the solution in each well was recorded at 490 nm by using a microplate reader.

The FTIR spectra of HMA, PHMA, CPDs-HMA, and CPDs-PHMA were depicted in Fig. 1 A. For HMA, the doublet peaks located at 3402 cm−1 and 3294 cm−1 were attributed to the N-H and O-H bonds. The N-H bond was further confirmed by the peak centered at 1541 cm−1 which related to the in-plane bending vibration. The absorptive bands at 3058 cm−1 and 2967 cm−1 were belonged to the C–H bond originating from vinyl and nitrohydroxymethyl groups, respectively. The peaks at 1668 cm−1 and 1627 cm−1 were the characteristic absorption bands of C=O and C=C bonds. The peaks appearing at 1410 cm−1 and 1016 cm−1 could be ascribed to the C–N and C–O bonds belonging to the N-hydroxymethyl unit.

In the FTIR spectra of PHMA, CPDs-HMA, and CPDs-PHMA, the peaks centered at 3058 cm−1 and 1627 cm−1 belonging to vinyl group disappeared, which indicated that the vinyl unit underwent a linear polymerization and formed one dimension chain structure. The absence of 1016 cm−1 corresponding to the C–O bond in CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA suggested the crosslinking reaction occurred between the hydroxymethyl groups, and the methylene bridge was formed by eliminating one water molecule and one formaldehyde molecule. A broad band observed between 3250 cm−1 and 3500 cm−1 indicated there existed intra/inter-molecular hydrogen bonding interaction between hydrogen atom with oxygen and nitrogen atom. Besides, there no conjugated moiety was found in the structure of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA, which was further confirmed by the absence of characteristic bands at 1360 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 in the Raman spectra which were assigned to D-band and G-band, respectively (Fig. 1B). Therefore, two-dimensional network structure formed by vinyl linear polymerization and hydroxymethyl crosslinking, together with the intra/inter-molecular hydrogen bonding, increased the rigidity of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA, resulting in a remarkable fluorescence emission. The relative quantum yields were determined as 4.35 % and 5.71 % for CPDs-HMA and CPDs- PHMA (Fig. S1), respectively. MTT experiments shown that the CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA was of low toxicity and good biocompatibility (Fig. S2).

In 1H NMR spectra (Fig. 1C), the signals at 5.6 ppm and 6.0 ppm belonging to the vinyl hydrogen of HMA disappeared in CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA, and new signals located in the range from 1.4 ppm to 2.7 ppm corresponding to methylene hydrogen appeared, revealing the vinyl polymerization occurred. The signals at 3.5 ppm and 4.6 ppm assigned to the hydroxymethyl hydrogen vanished, and new signal appeared at 3.0 ppm related to N, N′-methylene hydrogen indicated that crosslinking reaction was conducted between the hydroxymethyl groups. In 13C NMR spectra (Fig. 1D), the doublet signals centered at 128.3 ppm and 130.1 ppm ascribed to the vinyl carbon of HMA disappeared in CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA, and multiple peaks appeared in the range of 30–45 ppm corresponding to methylene carbon, demonstrating the vinyl polymerization. The signal at 168.7 ppm belonging to carbonyl carbon shifted downfield to 177.5 ppm and 181.6 ppm in CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA, which was ascribed to the disruption of the conjunction between vinyl group with carbonyl group owing to the vinyl polymerization. The signal at 62.3 ppm related to the hydroxymethyl carbon was not observed in CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA, and a new signal located at 57.8 ppm corresponded to N, N'-Methylene carbon, indicating the hydroxymethyl crosslinking occurred.

The full XPS spectra shown in Fig. 2 A, E clearly exhibited that CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA possessed three typical peaks which corroborating the presence of C, N, and O element. In the C 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2B, F), three signal peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.6 eV, and 288.2 eV were assigned to C–C, C–N/C–O, and C=O groups, respectively. The N 1s spectra (Fig. 2C, G) showed the existence of C–N (399.8 eV) and N-H (401.5 eV). Two O 1s bands centered at 531.6 eV and 533.4 eV were belonged to C=O and C–O bond (Fig. 2D, H), respectively. The lower contents of C–O bond probably came from the hydroxymethyl group on the edge surface of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA. The elemental composition ratios for C, N, and O in CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA were 67.25 %, 7.91 %, 24.84 % and 66.70 %, 6.21 %, 27.10 %, respectively. The high contents of carbon element indicated that HMA and PHMA underwent polymerization, crosslinking, and partial carbonization in the formation of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA. The slight difference of elemental contents reflected the minor differences of the chemical structure between the CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA.

As displayed in Fig. 3A, D, a single CPD exhibited a clear core–shell spherical microstructure. The inner core was an amorphous carbon core, and no lattice was observed. The out shell was a highly uniform crosslinking polymer layer. The core radius and shell thickness were about 30.46 nm, 13.41 nm and 56.31 nm, 24.11 nm for CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA, respectively. The average particle sizes of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA were about 52 nm and 63 nm (Fig. 3 C, F), respectively. Compared with the CPDs-PHMA prepared from PHMA, CPDs-HMA synthesized from HMA had a uniform size distribution. To our best knowledge, the clear core–shell morphology of the CPDs was observed for the first time.

The luminescence properties varied with the excitation wavelength and the concertation of the CPDs were investigated. The emission spectra of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA exhibited a strong excitation wavelength dependence (Fig. S3). The emission wavelength displayed an obvious red-shift with the excitation wavelength increasing and covered a broad spectral region from blue region to green region. It may attribute to the multiple photoluminescence centres which was considered to originate from the wide size distribution of the CPDs. The fluorescence spectra of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA varying with the concentration were measured and shown in Fig. 4 A and Fig. 4C. As displayed in Fig. 4B and Fig. 4D, at low concentration ranging from 0.025 mg/mL to 1.0 mg/mL, the emission intensity increased gradually with increase of the CPDs concentration, no remarkable change in the maximum emission wavelength was observed which located at 394 nm and 380 nm for CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA, respectively. With the concentration increasing from 2.5 mg/mL to 15.0 mg/mL, the emission wavelengths were red-shifted obviously from 457 nm to 528 nm for CPDs-HMA and 444 nm to 512 nm for CPDs-PHMA, respectively. Moreover, the emission intensity reduced gradually with the concentration increasing.

The variations of the emission spectra with the concentration could be attributed to the aggregation of CPDs via hydrogen bonding originated from large amounts of carbonyl and amino groups at the polymer layer. At low concentration, the inter-particle distance was large, and the interaction between the CPDs was weak. The fluorescence intensity increased gradually with the increase of the concentration, and the maximum emission wavelength was not changed obviously. As the CPDs concentration increasing, the distance between the CPDs decreased, and the CPDs aggregated through the hydrogen bonding interaction, which increased the electron delocalization of the CPDs, resulting in the decrease of the energy gap. Therefore, the maximum emission wavelength was gradually red shifted along with the concentration increasing. The fluorescence intensity decreasing with increase of the concentration was owing to the self-absorption effect of the CPDs at high concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT investigation of formation progress and photoluminescence mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The formation progress and photoluminescence mechanism were illustrated by DFT investigations [27]. Owing to the carbon core had an amorphous character, we only investigated the effect of polymer shell on the formation progress and photoluminescence mechanism of the CPDs. The simplified molecule models constructed by 4, 8, and 12 HMA molecules were investigated. Structural optimization and frequency calculation of the intermediates and products produced from polymerization and crosslinking were conducted at the PBE1PBE-GD3BJ/6-31G(d) level. 6-311G(d, p) basic set was applied in the reaction free energy and frontier orbital energy calculations.

As an example of the structure model constructed by 4 HMA molecules (Fig. 5 ), the HMA molecule went through polymerization, dimerization through hydrogen bonding, crosslinking by dehydration and formaldehyde elimination, and finally produced a two-dimensional network structure. The polymerization and crosslinking reaction were the endothermic reaction, with the reaction molecules of HMA increasing from 4 to 12 (Fig. 5A, Fig. S4), the required reaction energies increased gradually from 8.72 kJ/mol to 53.82 kJ/mol. The energy gaps (EH-L) between the HOMO and LUMO orbitals reduced gradually with the degree of polymerization and crosslinking increasing from 4 to 12 (Table S1). The energy gaps (EH-L) decreased gradually from 6.702 eV to 5.279 eV with the number of reaction molecule increase, which led to the red-shift in the fluorescence emission spectra.

In the model structure of the CPDs (Fig. 5A), the distances between O atoms in the carbonyl groups and H atoms in the amine groups ranged from 2.06 Å to 2.19 Å, and the distances between carbonyl oxygen atoms and hydroxyl hydrogen atoms at the edge were between 2.07 Å and 2.20 Å, which were much shorter than the sum of the van der Waals radii of O and H atoms (2.72 Å) [28], indicating there existed strong intramolecular hydrogen bonding interaction. Hence, the two-dimensional network structure formed by polymerization and crosslinking enlarged the photon absorption cross section. The intramolecular hydrogen bonding not only extended the electron delocalization but also enhanced the structural rigidity and resulted in an observable fluorescence emission. As shown in Fig. 5B, the formation of CPDs aggregation through intermolecular hydrogen bonding interaction increased with the concentration increasing. The energy gaps (EH-L) decreased from 6.70 eV to 5.43 eV from the monomer to trimer, which resulted in the emission wavelength red shifting along with the increase of aggregation degree of CPDs at high concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance and possible sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Analytical performance of the as-synthesized CPDs toward TC was conducted. Firstly, the concentration of the CPDs was set at 0.025 mg/mL, then, the analytical conditions such as pH and response time were optimized, the optimum analytical condition (Fig. S5), pH = 7 and no response time, were achieved. Under the optical conditions, the fluorescence spectra of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA varying with the concentrations of TC were recorded and depicted in Fig. 6 A, 6E, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity of the CPDs decreased gradually with the increase of the TC concentration, and a good linearity was obtained between the value (F0/F − 1) and TC concentration in the range from 0.1 μM to 80.0 μM. The linear equation was well fitted and described as F0/F − 1 = 0.0247CTC + 0.0188 (R2 = 0.996) for CPDs-HMA and F0/F-1 = 0.0249CTC + 0.0355 (R2 = 0.997) for CPDs-PHMA, respectively (Fig. 6B, F), where F0 and F was the fluorescence intensity of the CPDs in the absence and presence of TC, and CTC was the TC concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) calculated using 3σ/K was 0.037 μM for CPDs-HMA and 0.050 μM for CPDs-PHMA, respectively, where σ was the standard deviation of blank solutions (n = 10) and K was the slope of linear equation. Compared with the reported fluorescence probes (Table S2), the fluorescence method based on CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA displayed a wide linear range and low LOD, showing a potential application in TC detection. The possible quenching mechanism was considered as FRET mechanism, which was confirmed by the criterions that the considerable overlap between the UV absorption spectrum of TC with the fluorescence emission spectra of the CPDs (Fig. 6C, G) and the fluorescence lifetime for CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA decreasing from 7.93 ns and 6.27 ns to 6.28 ns and 4.47 ns before and after adding TC (Fig. 6D, H).

Fluorescence quenching efficiencies of the potential interferents including some structure analogues (CPL, AMX, TOB, and VAN) and inorganic ions (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, and Fe3+) were investigated to evaluate the selectivity of CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA toward TC. As shown in Fig. 7 A, D, compared with the strong fluorescence quenching of the CPDs by TC, the interferents with the concentration 3 times higher than that of TC displayed a slight or ignorable quenching. To further illustrate the high selectivity of the CPDs toward TC instead of the structure analogues and inorganic ions, UV–vis absorption spectra of the structure analogues and the absorption spectra of the CPDs in the absence and presence of interfering ions were measured. As displayed in Fig. 7B, E, weak spectra overlap between the absorption spectra of the structure analogues with the emission spectrum of the CPDs resulted in a slight fluorescence quenching. The absorption spectra of the CPDs were almost unchanged in the absence and presence of inorganic ions (Fig. 7C, F), which indicated that there was ignorable interaction between the CPDs with the interfering ions, and caused minor changes in the fluorescence quenching of the CPDs.

The determinations of TC in real water samples (tap water, river water, and lake water) were carried out to assess the practical application. No TC was detected, and then three different spiked concentrations (1.00 μM, 15.0 μM, and 40.0 μM) were added in the real water samples. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries ranged from 96.0 % to 103.0 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 5) were between 0.30 % and 2.30 %, which indicated that the CPDs-HMA and CPDs-PHMA as a fluorescence probe has a potential application in the determination of TC in environmental water samples.

In conclusion, the as-synthesized CPDs derived from HMA and PHMA displayed a clear core–shell structure, and exhibited intriguing excitation wavelength and concentration dependent luminescence property. Spectral characteristics and DFT calculations disclosed the formation progress and photoluminescence mechanism of the CPDs, rigid structure formed by vinyl polymerization, hydroxymethyl crosslinking, and intra/inter-molecular hydrogen bonding interaction resulted in the fluorescence emission. The concentration dependent luminescence property originated from the aggregation of the CPDs at high concentration. The established method based on the CPDs fluorescence has the characteristics of rapidness, wide linearity, and high sensitivity and selectivity with potential application in the TC detection.

Zixun Zou: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Hailong Wang: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Yanbo Zeng: Data curation. Yiwen Yang: Formal analysis. Zulei Zhang: Investigation, Visualization. Hong Huang: Writing – review & editing. Hongyu Hu: Writing – review & editing. Lei Li: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Program for Public Welfare Technology of Zhejiang Province (LGF20B050001), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21677060), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LY20B050009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112866.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbonized polymer dots: a brand new perspective to recognize luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials
J. Phys. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Y. Tao, T. L. Feng, C. Y. Zheng, S. J. Zhu, B. Yang, Carbonized polymer dots: a brand new perspective to recognize luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials, J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 10 (2019) 5182-5188, https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.jpclett.9b01384.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evolution and synthesis of carbon dots: from carbon dots to carbonized polymer dots
Adv. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. L. Xia, S. J. Zhu, T. L. Feng, M. X. Yang, B. Yang, Evolution and synthesis of carbon dots: from carbon dots to carbonized polymer dots, Adv. Sci. 6 (2019) 1901316, https://doi.org/10.1002/advs.201901316.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis processes, photoluminescence mechanism, and the toxicity of amorphous or polymeric carbon dots
Acc. Chem. Res.
